title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered for a community translation service. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Elena, The Clinic Interpreter
Elena volunteered with the community translation service at a busy health clinic where new arrivals often understandably struggled to describe symptoms. [47]She learned to ask patients to repeat key details in their own words slowly after appointments, so she could check that advice had been understood correctly.[/47] Her training as a nurse in another country years earlier helped her recognise when a word-for-word translation was not enough. She was always careful never to add her own medical opinion. [48]The most difficult morning session involved explaining the difference between a routine blood test and an urgent hospital referral to a frightened father.[/48] Elena stayed calm throughout by slowing the conversation and drawing a simple timeline. She was relieved when the family returned later to thank the staff. Elena says interpreting in clinics is not about speaking beautifully; it is about making sure nobody leaves with dangerous confusion about treatment.
texts.B:
### Sam, The Housing Form Helper
Sam helped residents complete housing forms at the advice centre, translating instructions and explaining what supporting evidence they needed to provide. [49]He introduced a checklist after several applicants arrived with the wrong supporting documents and had to return another frustrating day.[/49] His experience working in a council office helped him understand the confusing official order of the questions. He found the emotional pressure heavier than the actual language work, because a missing form could delay repairs or badly needed temporary accommodation quickly. [50]His biggest mistake was assuming that a tenant understood the word “landlord” when her country used a completely different rental system.[/50] After that, he slowly explained concepts before translating terms. Sam kept spare stamped envelopes, photocopy cards and pens in a plastic box nearby. He says the service works best when practical barriers are removed quietly and early, before people have to ask for help in public again.
texts.C:
### Priya, The School Meeting Translator
Priya translated at formal school meetings for parents who wanted to support their children confidently but were nervous about official language and unfamiliar routines. [51]She was asked to join after a teacher noticed her helping another parent understand a complicated permission letter in the playground.[/51] Priya quickly learned that these meetings moved too fast unless she interrupted very politely and asked staff to pause. [52]The hardest part was translating criticism of a child’s behaviour without making the parent feel attacked or the teacher sound unnecessarily unfair.[/52] She prepared useful phrases at home beforehand and checked school vocabulary carefully with older bilingual pupils. Priya was delighted when one previously anxious mother later attended parents’ evening without needing support for every difficult sentence. She believes translation should build independence, not permanent dependence. Her proudest habit is leaving families with a short list of questions they can ask confidently next time themselves.
texts.D:
### Noor, The Emergency Message Coordinator
Noor coordinated rapid translations for emergency text messages during winter storms, power cuts and sudden road closures. [53]She created short templates in six languages because long official warnings were often ignored or misunderstood on small phone screens during bad weather.[/53] Her background in local journalism helped her remove unnecessary words while keeping the meaning precise and useful. [54]She was shaken when an early message about a flooded bridge failed to mention that pedestrians were also seriously at risk.[/54] Nobody was hurt that evening, but she changed the review process immediately that night immediately. Noor now asks two volunteers to check every urgent public message before it is sent publicly. [55]She also keeps a list of community leaders who can record short voice notes for people who do not read easily under pressure.[/55] [56]She wants the service to practise regularly before emergencies, rather than discovering problems during the next serious storm.[/56]

questions.question: checks understanding by asking people to restate important information after appointments?
questions.explanation: Elena asks patients to repeat key details in their own words after appointments.

questions.question: had to clarify the difference between a routine test and an urgent referral?
questions.explanation: Elena explained the difference between a blood test and an urgent hospital referral.

questions.question: created a document checklist after people repeatedly brought unsuitable paperwork?
questions.explanation: Sam introduced a checklist when applicants arrived with the wrong documents.

questions.question: realised that a familiar term might not match another country’s housing system?
questions.explanation: Sam assumed a tenant understood “landlord”, but her country used a different rental system.

questions.question: was recruited after informally helping someone understand a school document?
questions.explanation: Priya was asked to join after a teacher saw her explain a permission letter to another parent.

questions.question: had to translate negative feedback carefully so neither side seemed unfair?
questions.explanation: Priya found it hard to translate criticism without making the parent feel attacked or the teacher sound unfair.

questions.question: designed brief multilingual warnings because long phone messages were ineffective?
questions.explanation: Noor created short templates in six languages because long warnings were often ignored or misunderstood.

questions.question: changed procedures after a warning omitted an important safety risk?
questions.explanation: Noor changed the review process after a flooded-bridge message failed to mention pedestrian risk.

questions.question: keeps contacts who can provide audio versions for people with reading difficulties?
questions.explanation: Noor keeps a list of community leaders who can record voice notes.

questions.question: wants the service to rehearse before it is needed in real crises?
questions.explanation: Noor wants the service to practise before emergencies rather than discover problems during a storm.
